artifacts.content:
Dear Laura,

I’m propped up here at Harborview, listening to the machines beep and thinking it might actually be quieter than Ballard on a Sunday. David just left to beat the traffic back to Redmond. He keeps fussing over my glasses since I cracked the good pair in that silly fall at the apartment on the 16th – they’ve got tape on one side again. I told him to bring the backup pair from his house when he comes tomorrow; I don’t want a repeat of February 18 at Swedish Ballard when they tried to hand me something to read and I couldn’t see a word. Poor Dr. Patel ended up writing that I couldn’t really review anything because between the presyncope from the heart block and those broken lenses, I was useless.

All of this, of course, started back on January 16 when David dragged me into St. Catherine after the chest pressure wouldn’t let up. Two days later, on the 18th, they bundled me into that ALS ambulance from Room 512B on Westlake over here to Harborview Cardiac & Vascular Institute. Dr. Lewis was waiting, and they had me wired to every monitor you can imagine. I remember them fussing over the nitroglycerin drip and the heparin, making sure the blood pressure didn’t drop again into the 90s.

I keep thinking about how different this all feels from Robert’s last hospitalization. Back then, I was the one sitting in the visitor’s chair, not the one with pacing pads stuck to my chest. I’m grateful you were able to get time off from the counseling office in Tacoma. Maybe when you come up next weekend we can sneak a little lemon cake into this place and pretend it’s an early birthday.

Love you,
Mom

metadata.subject: Settling in after the ambulance ride

artifacts.content:
Hi David,

I’ve been lying here thinking about how many times we’ve done this heart dance now. Do you remember that stress test at UW Medical Center on 3/12/25, when my heart rate dropped into the low 30s four minutes in? Dr. Nguyen slapped those sticky pacing pads on me and kept me on the monitor for six hours. He told us then that any future hospital-to-hospital trips should be by ALS ambulance with pacing, just in case the high‑grade AV block came back. I guess he knew what he was talking about.

Between that, my kidneys grumbling since the contrast CT at Swedish First Hill on 8/27/23 (creatinine from 1.5 up to 2.3 – I still remember the numbers) and Dr. Lee’s lecture about not bouncing around “random ERs,” it makes sense they sent me straight from St. Catherine on Westlake to Harborview’s cardiac ICU this time. With the heparin and nitro drips, and them worrying about my ejection fraction, a taxi really isn’t an option.

You’ve already missed so much work at the software job in Redmond, staying overnight after the January NSTEMI and now shuttling back and forth again. Please don’t burn yourself out trying to be everywhere at once. Maybe talk to HR about all this unpaid time off? I hate feeling like a burden when you’ve got Sophie’s school concerts, that custody mess finally behind you, and whatever fire drill your team’s dealing with.

I’ll try to nap a bit before your usual 8 p.m. call. Hearing your voice at night steadies me more than all these machines.

Love,
Mom

Journal – evening, 2026-02-02

The nurses keep saying tomorrow will be “busy,” which I think is code for scary. They’re planning to move me by ambulance over to the main Harborview cardiac ICU on February 3 for that left heart catheterization Dr. Patel has been talking about since the January stress test showed the big anterior defect and my ejection fraction down at 30%. They said an electrophysiologist, Dr. Karen Liu, will be there to look at this intermittent high‑grade AV block and maybe do a PCI if they find a tight spot. There’s also a cardiothoracic surgeon, Dr. Matthew Ortiz, “on standby” in case I need bypass. I try not to think too hard about that part.

They mentioned putting in a Swan‑Ganz line so they can watch the pressures in my heart minute by minute. All of this reminds me of Robert’s time in the ICU. Back then I was mostly worried about whether I’d remembered to pay Puget Sound Energy, not whether I’d wake up with a new scar.

I keep circling back to Dr. Carver at Swedish Ballard changing my antidepressant in December. Between the confusion and those dark, passive thoughts that creep in late at night, Dr. Patel was firm that I shouldn’t be taking the bus or a Lyft alone to something like this. Hard to argue when I sometimes lose track of what day it is.

Note to self: if I’m too groggy afterward, have David ask about cardiac rehab options that won’t drown us in co‑pays. He’s already on unpaid leave again.

Dear Carolyn,

I’m up too early, listening to the rain on NW 64th and thinking about how much life has changed since we were girls in Yakima. Some of it is almost funny, if I squint. Like needing a front‑wheeled walker just to shuffle from the recliner to the kitchen because my diabetic neuropathy is so bad I can’t feel my feet. After that bathroom fall on 11/03/24 at the apartment, Dr. Patel at the St. Catherine diabetes clinic wrote that I can only stand 1–2 minutes before my legs give out. No more climbing into David’s Subaru like I’m 40.

Do you remember when I panicked in his car on I‑5 near the Mercer exit last October on the way to Virginia Mason? The EMTs said I nearly fainted just from being in a regular car without monitors. Dr. Helen Morris at Ballard Behavioral Health later put in her note that non‑medical transport isn’t safe for me when my heart is acting up. Between that and my kidneys still touchy after the contrast CT at Swedish First Hill back in August 2023 (creatinine jumped from 1.5 to 2.3 and I spent two days on the renal floor), Dr. Lee told Laura we should head straight by ambulance to a hospital that knows me if the chest pain flares again.

It all sounds so dramatic written out, but mostly my days are small: Bible study on the laptop Mondays, watching the chickadees at the feeder, knitting baby hats for the hospital. I still try to get a slow lap around the block when the sidewalks aren’t slick. Independence looks different now, but I’m stubborn enough to hang onto what I can.

Write when you can.

Love,
Maggie

Event: Transfer to Harborview – cardiac MRI + CT angio
Date: 2026-02-15 (tentative – “mid‑February” per Dr. Patel)
Time: To be determined by Harborview scheduling
Location: Harborview Medical Center – cardiac MRI suite / CT angiography
Attendees: Me; David if he can get off work

Notes:

– This is the follow‑up after the abnormal nuclear stress test in January 2026 at the smaller community hospital. They said it showed a large anterior ischemic defect and my ejection fraction down around 30%.
– Plan is to go by ambulance again, not bus or senior shuttle, because of the intermittent high‑grade AV block and the mess last time when I nearly passed out trying to sign papers during an episode.
– Purpose of visit: detailed viability imaging and plaque characterization so the Harborview multidisciplinary heart team (cardiology + surgery) can decide between high‑risk PCI and CABG.
– Ask specifically: will this change the plan Dr. Liu mentioned for the early‑March EP lab visit and possible permanent dual‑chamber pacemaker? I don’t want to bounce between too many procedures.
– Remember to tell them about: 
   • prior contrast reaction at Swedish First Hill (8/27/23, creatinine 1.5→2.3, two days IV fluids on renal floor).
   • vascular dementia diagnosis from Dr. Nandakumar at Virginia Mason Memory Clinic (May 2024) and the wandering to Safeway on NW Market in November 2024 – reason Laura insists on supervised/ambulance transport.
– Bring: updated med list from the fridge, notebook with blood sugars, and my better pair of glasses (no more cracked lenses like February 18).

Sticky note on fridge – Transport / safety reminders

1. If chest pressure comes back like January 16 (tight, heavy, with shortness of breath walking to kitchen): DO NOT call a cab or try the senior shuttle.
   – Call 911 and ask for ALS ambulance.
   – Tell them: history of intermittent high‑grade AV block, prior NSTEMI in 2019, on insulin for type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease stage 3 (baseline creatinine ~1.6, eGFR ~35).
   – Mention: UW stress test 3/12/25 where HR dropped into 30s and they used external pacing pads; Dr. Nguyen’s note about needing pacing‑capable transport.

2. Memory / mood:
   – Vascular dementia (Dr. Nandakumar, Virginia Mason Memory Clinic, May 2024) – sometimes get turned around even in Ballard (Safeway wandering Nov 2024).
   – Depression and anxiety worse since Robert’s death in March 2014.
   – Under care of Dr. Helen Carver at Swedish Ballard since Feb 2023; December 2025 medication change caused confusion and passive “wish I wouldn’t wake up” thoughts. Do NOT travel alone to big procedures.

3. Falls / balance:
   – Bathroom fall 11/03/24 at 2147 NW 64th – Dr. Patel (St. Catherine diabetes clinic) says I can only stand 1–2 minutes with the walker; essentially no feeling in feet.

4. If David or Laura aren’t here and APS or a social worker shows up (like after Mrs. Holt saw me fall in the lobby in Oct 2023):
   – Give them David’s and Laura’s numbers from the magnet.
   – It’s okay; they’re trying to keep me safe.

Hi Elena dear,

I loved your pictures from Bellingham – that gray sky over the bay looks just like Seattle, so you won’t get homesick for the weather at least. I’m still here at Harborview, though they’re talking about moving me back to a step‑down unit soon if the rhythm behaves.

You’d laugh at how many specialists I’ve collected. There’s the electrophysiologist, Dr. Liu, who keeps talking about bringing me back to the EP lab in early March for 24‑hour invasive rhythm monitoring and maybe a permanent dual‑chamber pacemaker if this high‑grade AV block keeps acting up. Then there’s the heart team that wants those fancy MRI and CT pictures in mid‑February because of that January stress test showing the big weak spot in the front of my heart and the ejection fraction down at 30%.

On top of that, Dr. Liu (a different Dr. Liu – nephrologist Samuel Lee from Swedish First Hill) still frets about my kidneys after that contrast CT in 2023 when my creatinine jumped from 1.5 to 2.3 and I needed two days of IV fluids. He told your mom that if my heart acts up, we should go straight by ambulance to a place that has my records instead of letting me wander into some random ER.

I know all of this sounds heavy, but my days still have ordinary bits: the chaplain dropped off a crossword book, and one of the nurses showed me how to make the TV play old black‑and‑white movies. When you’re home for spring break, maybe you can come by with your guitar and we’ll have our own little concert in whatever room they’ve parked me in.

Study hard, sleep some, and don’t forget to eat real food.

Love you always,
Nana
